scenario: UPI fraud PhonePe GPay Paytm Google Pay money transferred without permission, OTP fraud bank account hacked online, internet banking fraud, net banking scam, someone took money from my UPI wallet without permission, debit card fraud, unknown transaction in bank account, money debited without my knowledge, phishing link bank fraud, fake customer care call fraud, screen sharing scam, digital payment fraud, IMPS NEFT RTGS fraud, mobile banking fraud, online financial fraud, account hacked money stolen digitally.
title: Cyber Fraud / Online Financial Fraud
steps: Step 1: Immediately call your bank helpline and block your account/card
Step 2: File a complaint on the National Cyber Crime Portal (cybercrime.gov.in)
Step 3: Visit your nearest police station and file an FIR under IT Act
Step 4: Collect all transaction screenshots, SMS alerts, and bank statements
Step 5: Contact your bank's fraud department and request a chargeback
Step 6: Follow up with cyber cell every 15 days until resolved
documents: Bank account statement showing fraudulent transaction
Screenshots of the transaction or suspicious messages
provisions: Section 66D of IT Act 2000 (Cheating by Impersonation)
RBI Circular on Zero Liability for Unauthorised Transactions
authority: Cyber Crime Cell (cybercrime.gov.in) → Local Police Station → Banking Ombudsman

scenario: Neighbour illegally encroaching on my land property boundary, someone building on my plot, illegal construction on my land, land boundary wall dispute with neighbour, property grabbed by relative or neighbour, my land occupied without permission, plot encroachment.
title: Property Dispute / Land Encroachment
steps: Step 1: Gather all original property documents (sale deed, tax receipts)
Step 2: Get a fresh survey done by a licensed surveyor to establish boundaries
Step 3: Send a legal notice to the encroaching party through a lawyer
Step 4: Approach the local Tahsildar / Revenue Authority to record the dispute
Step 5: File a civil suit for injunction in the Civil Court if unresolved
Step 6: Hire a civil lawyer for court representation
authority: Local Tahsildar Office → Civil Court → District Court

scenario: I bought a defective product online or from a shop, company is not giving refund, seller refusing to replace damaged goods, poor service, misleading advertisement, e-commerce fraud.
title: Consumer Complaint / Defective Product or Service
steps: Step 1: Contact the seller/company's customer care and demand refund in writing
Step 2: Send a formal legal notice via registered post
Step 3: File a complaint on the National Consumer Helpline (1800-11-4000)
Step 4: Register complaint on consumerhelpline.gov.in
Step 5: Approach the District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission
Step 6: Claim compensation for defective product and mental harassment
documents: Screenshots of order confirmation and communication with seller
Written complaint sent to company (with delivery proof)
authority: National Consumer Helpline (1800-11-4000) → District Consumer Commission → State Consumer Commission

scenario: Boss colleague sexually harassing at workplace office, unwanted physical contact at work, inappropriate touching at office, hostile work environment gender discrimination, POSH act violation, sexual remarks comments at workplace, manager harassing female employee.
title: Workplace Harassment / Sexual Harassment
steps: Step 1: Report immediately to your company's Internal Complaints Committee (ICC)
Step 2: If no ICC exists, approach the Local Complaints Committee (LCC) of your district
Step 3: Document all incidents with dates, times, and witnesses
Step 4: File a police complaint under IPC sections if ICC is unresponsive
Step 5: Approach the Labour Commissioner if the employer is retaliating
Step 6: Consult a lawyer to file a civil suit for damages
documents: Written complaint with detailed account of incidents
Copies of messages, emails, or recordings if available
provisions: POSH Act 2013 (Prevention of Sexual Harassment at Workplace)
Section 509 IPC (Word or gesture intended to insult modesty)
Article 21 of Constitution (Right to live with dignity)
authority: Internal Complaints Committee (ICC) → Local Complaints Committee (LCC) → Police Station → Labour Commissioner

scenario: Cheque bounced dishonoured returned by bank, insufficient funds cheque, cheque given to me returned unpaid, bank returned cheque with memo, party gave me cheque that bounced, payment cheque failed, cheque dishonoured signature mismatch or account closed.
title: Cheque Bounce / Dishonour of Cheque
steps: Step 1: Obtain the cheque return memo from your bank immediately
Step 2: Send a legal demand notice to the drawer within 30 days of receiving the memo
Step 3: Give the drawer 15 days to make the payment
Step 4: If payment not made, file a complaint under Section 138 NI Act within 30 days
Step 5: Approach the Magistrate Court with all documents
Step 6: The court can order imprisonment up to 2 years and double the cheque amount as fine
documents: Copy of legal demand notice sent (with postal receipt)
Bank statement showing the deposit attempt
Any agreement or proof of why cheque was given
authority: Magistrate Court (Judicial Magistrate First Class) in the area where the cheque was presented

scenario: Landlord is not returning security deposit, tenant eviction dispute, illegal rent hike, landlord harassment, rental agreement violation, owner not giving back advance.
title: Landlord-Tenant Dispute / Rent Dispute
steps: Step 1: Send a written demand notice to landlord for the security deposit
Step 2: Document all communications with landlord (messages, emails, receipts)
Step 3: Approach the Rent Control Court or Rent Authority of your city
Step 4: File a complaint under the state Rent Control Act
Step 5: Approach consumer forum if services like maintenance were paid for
Step 6: File a civil suit for recovery of money if amount is significant
documents: Bank transfer records of rent payments
provisions: State Rent Control Act (varies by state)
Section 406 IPC (Criminal Breach of Trust) if deposit withheld illegally
authority: Rent Control Court → Civil Court → Consumer Forum (for service-related issues)

scenario: Divorce, marriage separation, matrimonial dispute, husband wife conflict, domestic violence, dowry harassment, cruelty by spouse or in-laws, marital problems.
title: Matrimonial Dispute / Divorce
steps: Step 1: Consult a family lawyer to understand grounds for divorce applicable to you
Step 2: Attempt mediation or counselling if mutual consent divorce is possible
Step 3: If domestic violence is involved, file an FIR and approach Protection Officer
Step 4: File for divorce petition in the Family Court of your jurisdiction
Step 5: Apply for interim maintenance if financially dependent
Step 6: Address child custody matters alongside divorce proceedings
documents: Aadhaar Card / PAN Card of both parties
Evidence of cruelty or grounds for divorce
provisions: Muslim Personal Law / Dissolution of Muslim Marriages Act 1939
Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005
Section 498A IPC (Cruelty by husband or relatives)
authority: Family Court → District Court → Protection Officer (for domestic violence)

scenario: Employer not paying salary, company withholding wages, delayed salary, wrongful termination, illegal firing, no experience letter or relieving letter, EPF not deposited.
title: Employment / Labour Dispute
steps: Step 1: Send a written complaint to your HR department and management
Step 2: File a complaint with the Labour Commissioner of your district
Step 3: Approach the Labour Court under the Industrial Disputes Act
Step 4: File EPF grievance on the EPFO portal (epfigms.gov.in) if PF not deposited
Step 5: Approach Payment of Wages Authority for unpaid salary
Step 6: File a civil suit for recovery of dues if amount is large
documents: Email or written communication with employer
authority: Labour Commissioner Office → Labour Court → Payment of Wages Authority → EPFO Grievance Portal

scenario: Police not registering FIR, police refusing to file case, cop demanding bribe, police misconduct, false case filed by police, wrongful arrest, custodial torture.
title: Police Complaint / Misconduct
steps: Step 1: Request FIR registration in writing at the police station and take acknowledgement
Step 2: If refused, send a written complaint to the Superintendent of Police (SP)
Step 3: Approach the Judicial Magistrate directly — they can order FIR registration
Step 4: File a complaint with the State Human Rights Commission if tortured or harassed
Step 5: Approach the High Court for a writ of mandamus to compel FIR registration
Step 6: File a complaint with the State Police Complaints Authority
documents: Written complaint submitted to police (with acknowledgement)
Evidence of the crime (photos, videos, witnesses)
provisions: Section 166A IPC (Police failure to record FIR is an offence)
Article 21 of Constitution (Right to life and personal liberty)
authority: Superintendent of Police (SP) → Judicial Magistrate → High Court → State Human Rights Commission

scenario: Medical negligence, doctor made a mistake during surgery, wrong treatment given, hospital denied emergency care, pharmaceutical error, patient rights violation.
title: Medical Negligence / Hospital Complaint
steps: Step 1: Collect all medical records, prescriptions, and bills immediately
Step 2: File a complaint with the hospital administration in writing
Step 3: File a complaint with the State Medical Council against the doctor
Step 4: Approach the Consumer Forum — medical services are covered under Consumer Protection Act
Step 5: File a criminal complaint if negligence caused death or grievous hurt
Step 6: Consult a medical negligence lawyer for civil suit and compensation
documents: All medical records, discharge summary, lab reports
Second medical opinion from another doctor
authority: Hospital Administration → State Medical Council → Consumer Forum → Civil Court

scenario: Right to Information RTI application rejected, RTI not answered, public information denied, government transparency issue, PIO not responding to RTI.
title: RTI (Right to Information) Complaint
steps: Step 1: File your RTI application with the Public Information Officer (PIO) of the concerned department
Step 2: If no response within 30 days, file a First Appeal with the First Appellate Authority
Step 3: If appeal is rejected or ignored, file a Second Appeal with the Central/State Information Commission
Step 4: Approach the High Court if Information Commission fails to act
Step 5: You can also file RTI online at rtionline.gov.in
documents: Copy of original RTI application filed
Postal receipt / acknowledgement of RTI submission
authority: Public Information Officer (PIO) → First Appellate Authority → Central/State Information Commission → High Court

scenario: Loan app recovery agents calling threatening harassing, digital loan app harassing contacts and family, threatening messages from loan app, microfinance company harassing for repayment, debt collector threatening, loan app accessed my contacts and sending abusive messages, instant loan app blackmailing with photos.
title: Illegal Loan App / Recovery Harassment
steps: Step 1: Block all contact from the harassing numbers and take screenshots of threats
Step 2: File a complaint on the cybercrime portal (cybercrime.gov.in)
Step 3: File a police FIR under IT Act and IPC for extortion/harassment
Step 4: Report the app to RBI (sachet.rbi.org.in) and Google Play Store
Step 5: File a complaint with the RBI Ombudsman if a registered NBFC is involved
Step 6: Approach consumer forum for mental harassment compensation
documents: Screenshots of threatening messages and calls
Screen recordings of the app and its demands
Bank statements showing the loan amount received and paid
List of people harassed by the recovery agents
provisions: RBI Fair Practices Code for Lenders
authority: Cyber Crime Cell → Police Station → RBI Ombudsman → Consumer Forum

scenario: Caste discrimination, untouchability, atrocity against SC ST community, caste based abuse or violence, dalit rights violation, scheduled caste harassment.
title: Caste Discrimination / SC-ST Atrocity
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the nearest police station — police MUST register under SC/ST Act
Step 2: If police refuse, approach the SP or DSP (SC/ST Cell) directly
Step 3: Contact the National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC)
Step 4: Approach the District Magistrate for immediate relief and protection
Step 5: Claim compensation under the SC/ST Act rules
Step 6: Consult a legal aid lawyer — free legal aid is available for SC/ST victims
authority: Police Station (SC/ST Cell) → Superintendent of Police → National Commission for Scheduled Castes → District Magistrate

scenario: Insurance claim rejected, health insurance denied, life insurance not paid, motor insurance claim refused, insurance company not settling claim, insurance fraud.
title: Insurance Claim Dispute
steps: Step 1: Send a written complaint to the insurance company grievance officer
Step 2: If unresolved within 15 days, escalate to the Insurance Ombudsman
Step 3: File a complaint with IRDAI (bimabharosa.irdai.gov.in)
Step 4: Approach the Consumer Forum for deficiency of service
Step 5: File a civil suit in court if claim amount is large
documents: All medical records / bills (for health insurance)
FIR and vehicle repair estimate (for motor insurance)
Death certificate and nominee ID (for life insurance)
authority: Insurance Company Grievance Officer → Insurance Ombudsman → IRDAI → Consumer Forum

scenario: Fake profile created with my photos, morphed images shared online, someone spreading lies about me on social media Facebook Instagram Twitter, reputation damaged online, cyberbullying harassment online, trolling abusive comments online, fake news about me shared, online stalking through social media, obscene messages sent online.
title: Cyberbullying / Online Defamation
steps: Step 1: Take screenshots of all defamatory content immediately
Step 2: Report the content to the platform (Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, etc.)
Step 3: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 4: File an FIR at the nearest cyber crime police station
Step 5: Send a legal notice to the person if their identity is known
Step 6: Approach court for injunction to remove the content
documents: Screenshots / screen recordings of defamatory content
URL links to the posts or profiles
Proof of your identity (to show you are the victim)
Any prior communication with the accused
provisions: Section 67 IT Act (Publishing obscene content online)
Section 507 IPC (Criminal intimidation by anonymous communication)
authority: Cyber Crime Portal (cybercrime.gov.in) → Cyber Crime Police Station → Civil Court (for injunction)

scenario: Bike motorcycle scooter stolen missing from parking or home, car vehicle stolen from road or parking lot, two wheeler missing, vehicle theft physical property stolen, auto theft, found my vehicle missing when I came out, vehicle not where I parked.
title: Vehicle Theft / Stolen Property
steps: Step 1: File an FIR immediately at the nearest police station — this is mandatory for insurance claim too
Step 2: Provide the vehicle registration number, chassis number, and engine number to police
Step 3: Inform your vehicle insurance company within 24-48 hours of the theft
Step 4: Submit the FIR copy and keys to the insurance company to process the claim
Step 5: Obtain a Non-Traceable Certificate from police if vehicle is not found within 90 days
Step 6: Submit Non-Traceable Certificate to insurance company to claim total loss settlement
provisions: Section 379 IPC (Theft — punishment up to 3 years)
Motor Vehicles Act 1988 (for insurance claim procedure)
Insurance Act 1938 (for settlement of stolen vehicle claim)
authority: Nearest Police Station (FIR) → Insurance Company → Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (if insurer denies claim)

scenario: Mobile phone physically stolen snatched from hand on road or bus, smartphone handset missing from pocket or bag, someone grabbed my phone while walking, two wheeler bike snatched my phone, pickpocket took phone, phone missing from home physically stolen, IMEI number stolen device, physical theft of mobile handset, phone stolen not hacked.
title: Mobile Phone Theft / Snatching
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the nearest police station with your phone's IMEI number
Step 2: Block your SIM card immediately by calling your telecom operator
Step 3: Report the IMEI to the Department of Telecom at ceir.gov.in to block the phone nationally
Step 4: Change passwords of all accounts linked to the phone (Google, banking apps, UPI)
Step 5: If banking apps were on the phone, inform your bank and freeze your account temporarily
Step 6: Track the phone using Google Find My Device or Apple Find My if still active
documents: Phone purchase bill or invoice (with IMEI number)
IMEI number (found on box or *#06# before theft)
provisions: Section 66C IT Act (if accounts are misused after theft)
CEIR System — Dept of Telecom for blocking stolen phones
authority: Nearest Police Station → Telecom Operator (SIM block) → ceir.gov.in (IMEI block) → Cyber Cell (if accounts misused)

scenario: House burgled robbed, items stolen from home during break in, gold jewellery cash stolen from house, someone entered my house and stole things, break in at home, lock broken and items taken, home robbery, thief entered house and stole valuables.
title: House Burglary / Robbery
steps: Step 1: Do not disturb the crime scene — call police immediately
Step 2: File an FIR at the nearest police station with full list of stolen items and their approximate value
Step 3: Take photographs of the broken locks, entry points, and disturbed areas as evidence
Step 4: Inform your neighbours to check if they saw anything suspicious
Step 5: Inform your home insurance company if you have a policy
Step 6: Follow up with the investigating officer regularly and get case diary updates
documents: List of stolen items with approximate value
Photographs of crime scene and point of entry
Purchase receipts or photos of stolen valuables (jewellery, electronics) if available
provisions: Section 380 IPC (Theft in dwelling house — up to 7 years)
Section 457 IPC (Lurking house trespass to commit offence)
Section 395 IPC (Dacoity — if 5 or more persons involved)
authority: Nearest Police Station → Local Crime Branch → Insurance Company (if insured)

scenario: Physical assault, someone beat me up, attacked on the road, neighbour attacked me, gang assault, grievous hurt caused, fight resulting in injury, bodily harm.
title: Physical Assault / Bodily Harm
steps: Step 1: Get immediate medical treatment and obtain a Medico-Legal Certificate (MLC) from the hospital
Step 2: File an FIR at the nearest police station with full details of the incident and attacker
Step 3: Preserve all evidence — torn clothes, photos of injuries, CCTV footage if available
Step 4: Get witness statements from people who saw the assault
Step 5: If police are unresponsive, approach the Judicial Magistrate directly
Step 6: Consult a lawyer to file a compensation suit for medical expenses and mental trauma
documents: CCTV footage if available near the incident location
provisions: Section 323 IPC (Punishment for voluntarily causing hurt)
Section 307 IPC (Attempt to murder — if life was threatened)
Section 354 IPC (Assault on woman with intent to outrage modesty)
authority: Nearest Police Station → Judicial Magistrate (if FIR refused) → Civil Court (for compensation)

scenario: Someone blackmailing me threatening to share photos or videos, extortion demand for money with threat, threatening calls messages WhatsApp, being threatened by someone in person or online, ransom demand, person threatening to harm me or family if I dont pay, coercion.
title: Blackmail / Extortion / Threatening
steps: Step 1: Do NOT pay the blackmailer — it will only lead to more demands
Step 2: Save all threatening messages, calls, and evidence without responding
Step 3: File an FIR at the nearest police station or cyber crime cell
Step 4: If threatened online, report on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 5: Apply for anticipatory bail if you fear arrest due to false threats
Step 6: Seek a restraining order from court if the threat is physical
documents: Screenshots or recordings of threatening messages and calls
Evidence of extortion demand (amount, method demanded)
Any prior relationship with the blackmailer (if known)
provisions: Section 384 IPC (Punishment for extortion — up to 3 years)
authority: Nearest Police Station → Cyber Crime Cell (if online) → Judicial Magistrate → High Court (for protection order)

scenario: Land registration fraud, fake property documents, bogus sale deed, forged property papers, someone sold my property without my knowledge, duplicate registration of land.
title: Property Registration Fraud / Fake Documents
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the police station under IPC forgery sections
Step 2: Approach the Sub-Registrar office to flag the fraudulent registration
Step 3: File a civil suit for declaration that the fraudulent document is void
Step 4: Apply for stay order from court to prevent further transfer of property
Step 5: Approach the Inspector General of Registration with complaint
Step 6: Consult a property lawyer for recovery of possession
documents: Original sale deed and title documents
authority: Police Station → Sub-Registrar Office → Civil Court → Inspector General of Registration

scenario: Noise pollution from neighbour, factory, or construction site. Loud music from nearby shop or party. Loudspeaker nuisance at night. Industrial noise affecting daily life.
title: Noise Pollution / Nuisance Complaint
steps: Step 1: Document the noise with recordings and note timings and decibel levels if possible
Step 2: Send a written complaint to the local municipal authority or panchayat
Step 3: File a complaint with the local police under public nuisance laws
Step 4: Approach the State Pollution Control Board for industrial noise
Step 5: File a complaint in the Executive Magistrate court for noise nuisance
Step 6: File a civil suit for permanent injunction if unresolved
documents: Medical records if health is affected
provisions: Section 133 CrPC (Conditional order for nuisance removal)
authority: Local Police Station → Municipal Corporation → State Pollution Control Board → Executive Magistrate

scenario: Air pollution from factory or industry, water contamination by factory waste, chemical dumping in river or land, environmental damage by company, industrial effluent discharge.
title: Environmental Pollution / Industrial Damage
steps: Step 1: Document the pollution with photographs, videos, and water/air samples
Step 2: File a complaint with the State Pollution Control Board
Step 3: Report to the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)
Step 4: File a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) in High Court if large-scale damage
Step 5: Approach the National Green Tribunal (NGT) for environmental relief
Step 6: Claim compensation for health and property damage
documents: Water or soil sample test reports
provisions: Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974
Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1981
authority: State Pollution Control Board → Central Pollution Control Board → National Green Tribunal (NGT) → High Court (PIL)

scenario: Road accident, vehicle collision, hit and run case, car accident injury, motorcycle accident claim, accident compensation, third party insurance claim after road mishap.
title: Road Accident / Motor Vehicle Accident Claim
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the nearest police station immediately
Step 2: Seek medical treatment and obtain all medical records and bills
Step 3: Note the vehicle number of the other party and take photographs of the accident scene
Step 4: File a claim petition at the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT)
Step 5: Submit FIR, medical records, and injury photos to the insurance company
Step 6: If hit and run, claim compensation from Solatium Fund through the insurer
documents: Photographs of accident scene and injuries
provisions: Motor Vehicles Act Section 161 (Hit and run compensation)
Fatal Accidents Act 1855 (in case of death)
authority: Police Station (FIR) → Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (MACT) → Insurance Company → High Court (appeal)

scenario: Passport not issued, passport application rejected, passport office delaying, emergency passport needed, police verification pending for passport, name correction in passport.
title: Passport Issues / Delay or Rejection
steps: Step 1: Check your application status on passportindia.gov.in
Step 2: If police verification is pending, contact your local police station
Step 3: File a grievance on the Passport Seva portal online
Step 4: Visit the Passport Seva Kendra and speak to the Grievance Officer
Step 5: Write to the Regional Passport Officer with all supporting documents
Step 6: Approach the High Court for mandamus if urgently needed
documents: Proof of date of birth (birth certificate, school certificate)
provisions: Right to travel is a fundamental right under Article 21 of Constitution
authority: Passport Seva Kendra → Regional Passport Officer → Ministry of External Affairs Grievance → High Court

scenario: Aadhaar card update problem, name or date of birth correction in Aadhaar, Aadhaar biometric failure, Aadhaar linking issues with bank or mobile, Aadhaar enrollment rejected.
title: Aadhaar Card Issues / UIDAI Complaint
steps: Step 1: Visit the nearest Aadhaar Enrollment Centre or Common Service Centre (CSC)
Step 2: Submit a correction request online at myaadhaar.uidai.gov.in
Step 3: File a complaint on the UIDAI portal or call 1947 helpline
Step 4: Write to the Regional UIDAI Office with supporting documents
Step 5: If rights are being denied due to Aadhaar issues, approach a legal aid centre
documents: Supporting document for correction (birth certificate, school certificate, PAN card)
Enrollment ID or update request number
provisions: Aadhaar (Targeted Delivery of Financial and Other Subsidies) Act 2016
Supreme Court judgment on Aadhaar — Puttaswamy case 2018
authority: UIDAI Helpline (1947) → Enrollment Centre → Regional UIDAI Office → UIDAI Grievance Portal

scenario: PAN card not received, PAN card correction needed, wrong name or date of birth on PAN, duplicate PAN card issued, PAN card linking with Aadhaar issues.
title: PAN Card Issues / Income Tax ID Problem
steps: Step 1: Visit the NSDL or UTIITSL portal to check your PAN application status
Step 2: Submit a correction request on tin.tin.nsdl.com or utiitsl.com
Step 3: Call the Income Tax helpline at 1800-180-1961
Step 4: File a grievance on the Income Tax e-filing portal (incometax.gov.in)
Step 5: Visit your nearest Income Tax office with original documents for complex corrections
documents: Proof of date of birth (birth certificate)
authority: NSDL/UTIITSL Portal → Income Tax Helpline (1800-180-1961) → Income Tax Office → IT Ombudsman

scenario: Driving licence not issued, DL test failed unfairly, driving licence renewal delayed, name or address correction in DL, learner licence issues, traffic police seized my DL wrongly.
title: Driving Licence Issues
steps: Step 1: Check your application status on the Parivahan portal (parivahan.gov.in)
Step 2: File a grievance on the Parivahan Sewa portal
Step 3: Visit the Regional Transport Office (RTO) with all original documents
Step 4: If DL was wrongly seized by traffic police, file a complaint with the RTO or SP
Step 5: Approach the Transport Commissioner if RTO is unresponsive
provisions: Motor Vehicles Act 1988 (Chapter II — Licensing of drivers)
authority: Regional Transport Office (RTO) → Transport Commissioner → Parivahan Sewa Grievance Portal

scenario: Electricity bill is very high or wrong, inflated electricity bill, power supply disconnected without notice, electricity meter tampered by officer, new electricity connection delayed.
title: Electricity Bill Dispute / Power Supply Issue
steps: Step 1: Submit a written complaint to the local electricity distribution office
Step 2: Request a meter inspection and reading verification
Step 3: File a complaint on the state electricity board's grievance portal
Step 4: Approach the Consumer Grievance Redressal Forum of the electricity board
Step 5: Escalate to the Electricity Ombudsman if unresolved within 30 days
Step 6: File a complaint with the state Electricity Regulatory Commission
documents: Written complaint to electricity office (with acknowledgement)
authority: Electricity Distribution Office → Consumer Grievance Redressal Forum → Electricity Ombudsman → State Electricity Regulatory Commission

scenario: Gas cylinder not delivered, LPG subsidy not received in account, gas connection transfer problem, KYC for gas connection rejected, Ujjwala scheme connection issue.
title: LPG / Gas Connection Complaint
steps: Step 1: Contact your LPG distributor and register a written complaint
Step 2: Call the oil company helpline (IndianOil: 1800-2333-555, HP: 1800-233-3555, Bharat: 1800-22-4344)
Step 3: File a complaint on the Grievance Portal of the respective oil company
Step 4: For subsidy issues, check your bank account and contact your bank
Step 5: File a complaint at PGIMS (publicgrievances.gov.in) if unresolved
Step 6: Approach Consumer Forum for service deficiency
provisions: DBTL (Direct Benefit Transfer for LPG) Scheme
Petroleum and Natural Gas Regulatory Board Act
authority: LPG Distributor → Oil Company Helpline → PGIMS Grievance Portal → Consumer Forum

scenario: Ration card issues, ration not given at fair price shop, ration card not made, wrong entries in ration card, dealer not giving full ration, PDS corruption.
title: Ration Card / PDS Complaint
steps: Step 1: File a complaint with the local Fair Price Shop (FPS) supervisor
Step 2: Approach the Taluk or Block Supply Officer with a written complaint
Step 3: File a complaint on the state food department's grievance portal
Step 4: Contact the District Supply Officer (DSO) for non-issuance of ration card
Step 5: File an RTI to get information about your ration card status
Step 6: Approach consumer forum or legal aid if ration is being systematically denied
documents: Aadhaar Card of all family members
Proof of residence (utility bill, rent agreement)
authority: Fair Price Shop Supervisor → Block Supply Officer → District Supply Officer → State Food Department Grievance Portal

scenario: Bank not giving loan, loan application rejected unfairly, bank harassing loan defaulter, home loan fraud, personal loan trapped, bank not releasing property documents after loan closure.
title: Bank Loan Dispute
steps: Step 1: Send a written complaint to the bank's branch manager
Step 2: Escalate to the bank's internal grievance officer
Step 3: If unresolved within 30 days, approach the Banking Ombudsman
Step 4: File a complaint on the RBI Sachet portal (sachet.rbi.org.in)
Step 5: Approach Consumer Forum for deficiency of banking service
Step 6: Consult a lawyer for recovery of property documents or wrongful loan action
documents: Loan closure certificate (if loan is paid)
provisions: RBI Guidelines on Fair Practices Code for Lenders
SARFAESI Act 2002 (if property is being seized)
authority: Bank Branch Manager → Banking Ombudsman → RBI Sachet Portal → Consumer Forum → Debt Recovery Tribunal

scenario: ATM card cloned skimmed, money withdrawn from ATM without my knowledge, debit credit card cloned at ATM or shop, card fraud at point of sale, someone used my ATM card without permission, card details stolen at petrol pump or restaurant.
title: ATM Fraud / Card Cloning
steps: Step 1: Immediately call your bank's 24-hour helpline and block your debit/credit card
Step 2: File a written complaint with your bank within 3 days for zero liability benefit
Step 3: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 4: File an FIR at the nearest police station
Step 5: Request the bank for CCTV footage from the ATM
Step 6: Approach Banking Ombudsman if bank doesn't refund within 7 working days
documents: Bank account statement showing fraudulent transaction
Written complaint to bank with acknowledgement
provisions: RBI Circular on Limiting Liability of Customers in Unauthorised Electronic Banking Transactions
authority: Bank Helpline → Cyber Crime Portal → Police Station → Banking Ombudsman → Consumer Forum

scenario: Pension not received, government pension stopped, pension amount reduced, family pension not given after death of government employee, EPF pension dispute.
title: Pension Dispute / Non-Payment of Pension
steps: Step 1: Submit a written complaint to your pension disbursing authority (bank or treasury)
Step 2: Contact the office of the Head of Department where you were employed
Step 3: File a complaint on the Centralised Pension Grievance Redress and Monitoring System (CPENGRAMS)
Step 4: For EPF pension, file a complaint on the EPFO grievance portal
Step 5: Approach the Central Administrative Tribunal (CAT) for central government employees
Step 6: Approach the High Court if all other remedies are exhausted
authority: Pension Disbursing Authority → Head of Department → CPENGRAMS Portal → Central Administrative Tribunal → High Court

scenario: Child custody dispute, grandparent visitation rights, parental kidnapping, father not allowing mother to meet child, illegal adoption, custody after divorce.
title: Child Custody Dispute
steps: Step 1: File a petition for custody in the Family Court of your jurisdiction
Step 2: Apply for interim custody order while the main case is pending
Step 3: If child is taken away illegally, file an FIR for kidnapping
Step 4: File a habeas corpus petition in High Court for immediate custody
Step 5: Child's welfare and best interests are the primary consideration for courts
Step 6: Mediation through family court counsellors is often ordered first
documents: Aadhaar Cards of parents and child
Any prior court orders regarding custody
provisions: Article 21 of Constitution (child's right to life and dignity)
authority: Family Court → High Court (Habeas Corpus) → Police Station (if kidnapping)

scenario: Inheritance dispute, will is being challenged, property not given to legal heir, probate of will contested, ancestral property denied, succession certificate needed.
title: Inheritance / Will Dispute
steps: Step 1: Obtain a copy of the will from the Sub-Registrar office if registered
Step 2: Apply for a Succession Certificate from the District Court
Step 3: If will is disputed, file a probate petition in the District or High Court
Step 4: Apply for Letters of Administration if there is no will
Step 5: File a civil suit for partition of ancestral property if denied your share
Step 6: Consult a succession lawyer immediately as time limits apply
authority: Sub-Registrar Office → District Court → High Court (for probate) → Civil Court (partition suit)

scenario: Husband beating wife at home, physical abuse by spouse partner, in-laws torturing emotionally or physically, partner threatening violence, domestic abuse at home, wife beaten by husband, family violence at home, need protection order from abusive partner or spouse.
title: Domestic Violence
steps: Step 1: Call Women's Helpline (181) or National Domestic Violence Helpline (1091) immediately
Step 2: Approach the Protection Officer in your district for emergency help
Step 3: File an application for Protection Order under DV Act in the Magistrate Court
Step 4: Apply for an emergency residence order to stay in the shared household
Step 5: Get a Medico-Legal Certificate for all injuries from a government hospital
Step 6: File FIR under Section 498A IPC for cruelty
documents: Witness statements from neighbours or family
provisions: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005
Section 498A IPC (Cruelty by husband and relatives)
authority: Women's Helpline (181) → Protection Officer → Magistrate Court (Protection Order) → Police Station (FIR)

scenario: Someone following me everywhere on road or to college office, unknown person stalking tracking my location physically, obsessive person outside my house waiting for me, being followed by ex-partner or stranger persistently, person showing up everywhere I go, physical stalking harassment.
title: Stalking / Persistent Harassment
steps: Step 1: Document every stalking incident with date, time, and location
Step 2: File an FIR at the nearest police station under Section 354D IPC
Step 3: Inform your family, friends, and workplace security about the stalker
Step 4: Apply for a restraining order or protection order from the Magistrate
Step 5: Save all evidence — messages, emails, photos of the stalker
Step 6: If stalking is online, also file on cybercrime.gov.in
documents: Diary of incidents with dates and times
Screenshots of messages or online contact by stalker
Photographs or CCTV footage of stalker
provisions: Section 354D IPC (Stalking — up to 5 years for repeat offence)
Section 507 IPC (Criminal intimidation by anonymous communication)
Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005
authority: Nearest Police Station → Judicial Magistrate (Protection Order) → Cyber Crime Cell (if online)

scenario: Child labour, minor working in factory or shop, child being forced to work, child not sent to school by parents, child trafficking, bonded child labour.
title: Child Labour / Child Rights Violation
steps: Step 1: Call Childline 1098 immediately — available 24/7 for child protection
Step 2: Report to the local Child Welfare Committee (CWC)
Step 3: File a complaint with the Labour Department
Step 4: File an FIR at the police station — child labour is a criminal offence
Step 5: Contact the District Child Protection Officer (DCPO)
Step 6: Report to the National Commission for Protection of Child Rights (NCPCR)
documents: Photographs or videos of child working (if safely obtainable)
provisions: Child Labour (Prohibition and Regulation) Amendment Act 2016
Article 24 of Constitution (prohibition of child labour)
authority: Childline 1098 → Child Welfare Committee → Labour Department → Police Station → NCPCR

scenario: Land acquisition by government without compensation, government taking private land for project, inadequate compensation for acquired land, delayed compensation for government land acquisition.
title: Land Acquisition Dispute / Government Compensation
steps: Step 1: Review the Land Acquisition notice and verify the purpose and compensation offered
Step 2: File objections within the deadline mentioned in the acquisition notice
Step 3: Attend the public hearing conducted by the Land Acquisition Officer
Step 4: If compensation is inadequate, file a reference to the Land Acquisition Court
Step 5: Appeal to the High Court if Land Acquisition Court order is unsatisfactory
Step 6: Consult a lawyer who specialises in land acquisition matters immediately
provisions: Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition Act 2013
authority: Land Acquisition Officer → Land Acquisition Court → High Court → Supreme Court

scenario: GST not filed correctly, GST notice received from tax department, wrong GST charged by seller, GST refund not received, GST registration cancelled wrongly, fake GST bill.
title: GST Dispute / Tax Notice
steps: Step 1: Read the GST notice carefully and note the deadline for reply
Step 2: Consult a CA or tax lawyer immediately for complex notices
Step 3: File a reply to the GST notice on the GST portal (gst.gov.in)
Step 4: For wrongly charged GST by seller, file a complaint on the GST portal
Step 5: Approach the GST Appellate Authority if order is against you
Step 6: Approach High Court for writ if fundamental rights are violated
provisions: Central Goods and Services Tax Act 2017
GST Appeal and Revision provisions (Chapter XVIII)
Income Tax Act 1961 (for related tax matters)
authority: GST Portal → GST Officer → GST Appellate Authority → GST Appellate Tribunal → High Court

scenario: Income tax notice, IT department notice for tax evasion, wrong income tax assessment, TDS not reflected in Form 26AS, income tax refund not received, scrutiny notice.
title: Income Tax Notice / Dispute
steps: Step 1: Read the income tax notice carefully and identify the section under which it is issued
Step 2: Consult a CA or tax lawyer immediately — time limits for reply are strict
Step 3: File a reply on the income tax e-filing portal (incometax.gov.in)
Step 4: If refund is pending, check on the portal and file a grievance if delayed
Step 5: Approach the Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) if assessment order is wrong
Step 6: Appeal to the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) if needed
documents: Income tax returns filed for relevant years
authority: Income Tax Officer → Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) → ITAT → High Court

scenario: Builder not giving possession of flat, real estate fraud, builder taking money and not completing project, RERA complaint against builder, delay in flat possession, builder refusing to refund.
title: Real Estate / Builder Dispute (RERA)
steps: Step 1: Send a legal notice to the builder demanding possession or refund within 30 days
Step 2: File a complaint on your state's RERA portal (e.g. maharera.mahaonline.gov.in)
Step 3: RERA Adjudicating Officer can order refund with 10.85% interest
Step 4: Approach Consumer Forum if RERA is unresponsive
Step 5: File a criminal complaint if money is fraudulently taken without any intent to complete project
Step 6: Join with other flat buyers for collective RERA complaint for stronger impact
documents: Brochure and flat plan shared by builder
RERA registration number of the project
provisions: Section 18 RERA (Return of amount and compensation)
authority: State RERA Authority → RERA Adjudicating Officer → Consumer Forum → Civil Court

scenario: Fake degree or certificate, forged educational documents, college not issuing original certificate, university delaying marksheet or degree, migration certificate not given.
title: Educational Certificate Fraud / Non-Issue
steps: Step 1: Send a written application to the college or university demanding the certificate
Step 2: File a complaint with the University Grants Commission (UGC) if a UGC-recognised institution
Step 3: Approach the state education department for intervention
Step 4: File an FIR if documents are forged or fraudulently withheld
Step 5: Approach the High Court for a writ of mandamus compelling issue of certificates
documents: Original admission documents and fee receipts
authority: College / University → UGC → State Education Department → High Court

scenario: Hostel facility problems, hostel warden misbehaving, college hostel food quality issues, sexual harassment in hostel, hostel room allotment problems, unfair hostel fee.
title: College Hostel Complaint
steps: Step 1: Submit a written complaint to the Hostel Warden and Chief Warden
Step 2: Escalate to the Dean of Students or Registrar of the institution
Step 3: If sexual harassment, report to the Internal Complaints Committee (ICC)
Step 4: File a complaint with the UGC or AICTE if the institution is unresponsive
Step 5: Approach the State Human Rights Commission if rights are violated
documents: Hostel admission documents and fee receipts
Evidence of harassment or poor facilities (photos, messages)
provisions: UGC Regulations on Curbing Menace of Ragging
Consumer Protection Act 2019 (hostel as paid service)
authority: Hostel Warden → Dean of Students → UGC/AICTE → NHRC/State HRC

scenario: Ragging in college or university, senior students bullying or harassing juniors, physical or mental ragging, eve teasing in educational institution.
title: Ragging / Bullying in Educational Institution
steps: Step 1: Report immediately to the Anti-Ragging Committee of the institution
Step 2: Call the UGC Anti-Ragging Helpline: 1800-180-5522
Step 3: File an FIR at the nearest police station — ragging is a criminal offence
Step 4: File a complaint on antiragging.in portal
Step 5: The institution is liable to expel the accused and compensate the victim
provisions: UGC Regulations on Curbing the Menace of Ragging 2009
Supreme Court guidelines in SLP No. 24295 of 2006
authority: Anti-Ragging Committee → UGC Helpline (1800-180-5522) → Police Station → antiragging.in

scenario: Voter ID card not received, name missing from voter list, wrongly deleted from electoral roll, voter ID correction needed, unable to vote due to Voter ID issue.
title: Voter ID / Electoral Roll Issue
steps: Step 1: Check your name on the voter list at nvsp.in or voterportal.eci.gov.in
Step 2: File Form 6 for new registration or Form 8 for corrections online or at ERO office
Step 3: Track your application status on the voter portal
Step 4: Contact your local Electoral Registration Officer (ERO) for unresolved issues
Step 5: File a complaint with the Election Commission of India if the ERO is unresponsive
documents: Proof of residence (utility bill, bank statement)
authority: Electoral Registration Officer (ERO) → District Election Officer → Election Commission of India

scenario: Caste certificate not issued, OBC certificate rejected, SC ST certificate application pending, fake caste certificate issued to someone, caste certificate needed for government job or college.
title: Caste Certificate / OBC Certificate Issue
steps: Step 1: Apply through your state's e-district or revenue department portal
Step 2: Submit application with required documents to the Tahsildar or SDM office
Step 3: Follow up and get acknowledgement of your application
Step 4: If delayed beyond stipulated time, file a complaint with the District Collector
Step 5: File an RTI to get information on your application status
Step 6: Approach the High Court if certificate is wrongly denied
documents: Parent's caste certificate or school records
provisions: Relevant state laws on backward classes
authority: Tahsildar / SDM Office → District Collector → State Backward Classes Commission → High Court

scenario: Disability certificate not issued, person with disability denied benefits, accessible infrastructure not provided, disability discrimination in workplace or public place, RPWD Act violation.
title: Disability Rights / PwD Certificate Issue
steps: Step 1: Apply for disability certificate at the designated government hospital in your district
Step 2: File a complaint with the Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities
Step 3: Approach the State Commissioner for Disabilities
Step 4: For workplace discrimination, file complaint with Labour Commissioner and under RPWD Act
Step 5: Approach the High Court if rights are denied
documents: Evidence of discrimination or denial of benefits
provisions: Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act 2016 (RPWD Act)
Article 41 of Constitution (right to public assistance)
authority: District Medical Board → State Commissioner for Disabilities → Chief Commissioner for Disabilities → High Court

scenario: Senior citizen being abused by children, elderly person thrown out of home, maintenance not paid to parents, aged parent neglect, old age pension not given.
title: Senior Citizen Abuse / Maintenance by Children
steps: Step 1: Call the Senior Citizens Helpline: 14567
Step 2: File a complaint with the Maintenance Tribunal under the Senior Citizens Act
Step 3: The Tribunal can order children to pay up to Rs. 10,000 per month maintenance
Step 4: File an FIR if physical abuse or property fraud is involved
Step 5: Approach the District Social Welfare Officer
Step 6: Contact Agewell Foundation or HelpAge India for support
documents: Property documents if property dispute is involved
Details of children (their addresses and income)
provisions: Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act 2007
Section 23 of Senior Citizens Act (property transferred under conditions)
authority: Senior Citizen Helpline (14567) → Maintenance Tribunal → Police Station → District Social Welfare Officer

scenario: Sexual assault on woman, rape, molestation, eve teasing, groping in public, unwanted physical contact, obscene gesture or words directed at woman.
title: Sexual Assault / Molestation
steps: Step 1: Call Women Helpline 181 or Police 112 immediately
Step 2: Seek immediate medical attention for treatment and evidence collection (do not bathe before medical examination)
Step 3: File an FIR at the nearest police station — police must register it immediately
Step 4: A female police officer must record the statement of the victim
Step 5: Free legal aid is available — contact the District Legal Services Authority (DLSA)
Step 6: The case will be tried in a Special Fast Track Court
documents: Clothing worn at the time of assault (preserved)
provisions: Section 354 IPC (Assault with intent to outrage modesty)
POCSO Act 2012 (if victim is a minor)
authority: Police Station → Women Helpline 181 → Special Fast Track Court → District Legal Services Authority (free legal aid)

scenario: Murder, culpable homicide, suspicious death in family, body found and police not investigating, death in custody, honour killing.
title: Murder / Suspicious Death Investigation
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the nearest police station immediately
Step 2: Insist on a post-mortem examination and obtain the post-mortem report
Step 3: If police are not investigating, approach the SP or DIG
Step 4: File a petition in the High Court for CBI or SIT investigation if local police are biased
Step 5: Hire a private lawyer to monitor and assist in the criminal trial
Step 6: File for compensation under victim compensation scheme
documents: Any evidence related to the case
provisions: Section 302 IPC (Murder — life imprisonment or death penalty)
Section 304 IPC (Culpable homicide not amounting to murder)
Section 176 CrPC (Magistrate's inquiry in case of death in custody)
authority: Police Station → Superintendent of Police → High Court (for CBI/SIT) → Fast Track Court

scenario: Online shopping fraud, product not delivered after payment, fake website took money, e-commerce scam, wrong product delivered and refund not given, marketplace seller fraud.
title: Online Shopping Fraud / E-Commerce Scam
steps: Step 1: Contact the e-commerce platform's customer support and demand refund or delivery
Step 2: File a complaint on the National Consumer Helpline (1800-11-4000)
Step 3: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 4: File a complaint on consumerhelpline.gov.in
Step 5: Raise a chargeback with your bank or payment provider
Step 6: Approach Consumer Forum for compensation
documents: Payment receipt or bank transaction proof
Communication with the seller or platform
Photographs of wrong or damaged product
authority: Platform Customer Support → National Consumer Helpline → Cyber Crime Portal → Consumer Forum

scenario: Matrimonial fraud, cheated through matrimonial site, person lied about job or marital status for marriage, fake marriage, dowry taken and then abandoned, NRI marriage fraud.
title: Matrimonial Fraud
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the police station for cheating and fraud under IPC
Step 2: If NRI is involved, report to the NRI Helpline and Ministry of External Affairs
Step 3: Approach the matrimonial website with a complaint about the fraudulent profile
Step 4: File for divorce on grounds of fraud in the Family Court
Step 5: Claim return of dowry and compensation through appropriate legal channels
documents: Marriage certificate or proof of marriage ceremony
Evidence of fraud (fake documents, screenshots of lies)
Dowry given proof (receipts, photos, bank transfers)
authority: Police Station → Family Court → NRI Helpline (MEA) → Consumer Forum (for matrimonial website fraud)

scenario: Drug addiction treatment needed, someone forcing drugs on minors, illegal drug supply in neighbourhood, drug peddler near school, narcotics trafficking, NDPS case.
title: Drug Abuse / Narcotics Complaint
steps: Step 1: Call the NDPS helpline or local police to report drug peddlers
Step 2: File an FIR with the police anti-narcotics cell
Step 3: For treatment, contact the nearest de-addiction centre or NIMHANS
Step 4: Report to the Narcotics Control Bureau (NCB) for large trafficking
Step 5: File a complaint with the school or college management if drugs are being supplied near educational institutions
documents: Evidence of drug activity (photographs, videos) if safely obtained
Aadhaar Card / ID proof of complainant
provisions: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (NDPS) Act 1985
Prevention of Illicit Traffic in Narcotic Drugs Act 1988
Section 27A NDPS (financing drug traffic — very severe punishment)
authority: Police Anti-Narcotics Cell → Narcotics Control Bureau (NCB) → De-addiction Centre (for treatment)

scenario: Arms and weapons illegally possessed by neighbour or enemy, illegal firearm threat, unlicensed gun, bomb threat, country-made weapon possession, ammunition found.
title: Illegal Weapons / Arms Complaint
steps: Step 1: Call 100 (Police emergency) or 112 immediately — do not confront the person
Step 2: File an FIR at the police station with all details
Step 3: If under immediate threat, seek protection from nearest police post
Step 4: Report to the District Magistrate who has powers under Arms Act
documents: Aadhaar Card / ID proof of complainant
Evidence of illegal weapon (photographs, videos if safely taken)
provisions: IPC sections for threat with weapon (Section 506, 307)
authority: Police Emergency (100/112) → Police Station → District Magistrate

scenario: Terrorism, bomb blast, terrorist attack, suspicious activity, radicalisation of youth, terror funding suspicion, anti-national activity.
title: Terrorism / Suspicious Activity Report
steps: Step 1: Call 100/112 immediately if there is immediate threat
Step 2: Contact the National Investigation Agency (NIA) helpline for terror-related complaints
Step 3: Report to state Anti-Terrorism Squad (ATS)
Step 4: For online radicalisation, report on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 5: Your identity will be kept confidential when reporting terror activities
documents: Evidence of suspicious activity (if safely obtained)
Aadhaar Card / ID proof (optional for anonymous reports)
authority: Police Emergency (100) → State ATS → National Investigation Agency (NIA) → Intelligence Bureau

scenario: Fake news, misinformation spread on WhatsApp or social media, morphed videos spreading false information, deepfake content, someone spreading rumours about me online.
title: Fake News / Misinformation / Deepfake
steps: Step 1: Report the fake content directly on the platform (WhatsApp, Facebook, etc.)
Step 2: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 3: File an FIR at the cyber crime police station
Step 4: If it affects your reputation, send a legal notice for defamation
Step 5: Approach court for injunction to stop spread of fake content
Step 6: Report to Press Council of India if it involves news media
documents: Screenshots of fake content with URL and date
Proof of your identity and that you are the victim
Evidence that the content is false
authority: Cyber Crime Portal → Police Station → Court (defamation/injunction) → Press Council of India

scenario: Government job recruitment fraud, fake job offer letter, money taken for government job, sarkari naukri scam, promise of job in exchange for bribe, employment scam.
title: Government Job / Recruitment Fraud
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the nearest police station for cheating and fraud
Step 2: Complaint to the recruiting authority (UPSC, SSC, State PSC, Railways etc.) about the fraud
Step 3: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in if the fraud happened online
Step 4: Report to the Anti-Corruption Bureau or Vigilance Department
Step 5: Approach consumer forum if money was paid to a fraudulent coaching institute
documents: Fake job offer letter or appointment letter
All payment receipts to the fraudster
Any documents submitted to the fraudster
provisions: Prevention of Corruption Act 1988 (if government official involved)
authority: Police Station → Anti-Corruption Bureau → Recruiting Authority (UPSC/SSC/PSC) → Cyber Crime Cell

scenario: Private money lender charging excessive interest, illegal moneylender harassing, usury, private financier seizing vehicle or property illegally, informal loan harassment.
title: Illegal Moneylender / Usury Complaint
steps: Step 1: File a complaint with the local police against the moneylender for extortion
Step 2: File a complaint with the District Collector — moneylenders need a licence
Step 3: File an FIR if the moneylender has illegally seized your property or vehicle
Step 4: Approach the RBI if an unregistered NBFC is involved
Step 5: Consult a lawyer for a civil suit to recover illegally seized property
documents: Loan agreement (if any written document exists)
Evidence of harassment or threats (screenshots, recordings)
provisions: State Money Lending Acts (each state has own law)
Section 385 IPC (Putting person in fear for extortion)
authority: Police Station → District Collector → State Money Lending Authority → RBI

scenario: WhatsApp or social media account hacked, email hacked, someone using my social media account, unauthorized access to my account, password stolen, two-factor authentication bypassed.
title: Social Media / Email Account Hacking
steps: Step 1: Immediately try to recover your account using official recovery options
Step 2: Report the hacked account to the platform (Facebook, Instagram, Google etc.)
Step 3: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 4: Change passwords of all linked accounts immediately
Step 5: File an FIR at the cyber crime police station
Step 6: Inform your contacts that your account was hacked to prevent further fraud
documents: Screenshots of suspicious activity on account
Mobile number and email ID linked to account
provisions: Section 43 IT Act (Damage to computer or data)
authority: Cyber Crime Portal (cybercrime.gov.in) → Platform Report → Cyber Crime Police Station

scenario: Water supply not provided by municipality, contaminated drinking water, borewell or water connection not given, water tanker supply stopped, public tap not working.
title: Water Supply / Municipal Complaint
steps: Step 1: File a written complaint with your local Municipal Corporation or Panchayat water department
Step 2: Contact the ward councillor or elected representative
Step 3: File a complaint on the municipal grievance portal
Step 4: Get water tested for contamination at a government lab
Step 5: Approach the State Water Resources Department
Step 6: File a PIL in High Court if large number of people are affected
provisions: Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974
Article 21 of Constitution (right to clean water)
authority: Municipal Corporation/Panchayat → State Water Department → State Pollution Control Board → High Court (PIL)

scenario: Road pothole caused accident, municipal road not repaired, broken road causing vehicle damage, footpath encroached upon, public road blocked illegally, street light not working.
title: Road / Municipal Infrastructure Complaint
steps: Step 1: File a complaint on your city's pothole reporting app or municipal portal
Step 2: Contact your ward councillor or MLA
Step 3: File a complaint with the PWD (Public Works Department)
Step 4: If accident caused by pothole, file FIR and MACT claim for compensation
Step 5: File a PIL in High Court if the issue affects large number of people
documents: Photographs and videos of road condition
Medical records and vehicle damage report (if accident)
provisions: Article 21 of Constitution (right to safe roads)
authority: Municipal Corporation → PWD → Ward Councillor → MACT (for accident) → High Court (PIL)

scenario: Hospital refusing to treat emergency patient, denial of medical treatment, not admitting patient without money, hospital demanding upfront payment for emergency, ambulance denied.
title: Hospital Refusing Emergency Treatment
steps: Step 1: Call 108 (ambulance) or 112 for emergency medical help
Step 2: Document the refusal — get names of staff who refused and time of refusal
Step 3: File an FIR — denial of emergency treatment is a criminal offence
Step 4: File a complaint with the State Medical Council
Step 5: File a complaint with the State Human Rights Commission
Step 6: Approach Consumer Forum for deficiency in emergency services
documents: Written record of refusal with names and time
Bills and payment demands made by hospital
provisions: Section 304 IPC (Culpable homicide if patient dies due to refusal)
authority: Police Station → State Medical Council → State Human Rights Commission → Consumer Forum

scenario: Fake medicine sold, spurious drug given at pharmacy, wrong medicine dispensed, expired drugs sold, drug substitution by chemist, pharmacy overcharging for medicines.
title: Fake / Spurious Medicine Complaint
steps: Step 1: Keep the medicine and its packaging as evidence — do not discard
Step 2: File a complaint with the Drug Inspector of your area
Step 3: Report to the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO)
Step 4: File a consumer complaint for defective product
Step 5: File an FIR if health damage was caused by fake medicine
documents: Medicine packaging with batch number and expiry date
Medical records if health was affected
provisions: Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940 (Section 27 — penalty for spurious drugs)
authority: District Drug Inspector → CDSCO → Police Station → Consumer Forum

scenario: Food adulteration, contaminated food sold in restaurant or shop, food poisoning from restaurant, foreign objects in packaged food, FSSAI standards violation.
title: Food Adulteration / Food Safety Complaint
steps: Step 1: Preserve the food sample as evidence in a sealed container
Step 2: Seek medical treatment and keep all records
Step 3: File a complaint with the local Food Safety Officer (FSO)
Step 4: Report to FSSAI (fssai.gov.in) — call 1800-11-2100
Step 5: File a consumer complaint for deficiency and damages
Step 6: File an FIR if the food adulteration caused serious harm
documents: Purchase receipt or bill from restaurant
authority: Local Food Safety Officer → FSSAI Helpline (1800-11-2100) → Police Station → Consumer Forum

scenario: Bribery demand by government official, public servant asking for money to do their job, corruption by government employee, tender fraud, public money embezzlement.
title: Bribery / Government Corruption
steps: Step 1: Do NOT pay the bribe — paying bribe is also an offence
Step 2: File a complaint with the Anti-Corruption Bureau (ACB) or Vigilance Department
Step 3: You can also set a trap with the ACB to catch the official red-handed
Step 4: File a complaint on the CVC (Central Vigilance Commission) portal for central government officials
Step 5: File an RTI to expose corruption and get information denied to you
documents: Evidence of bribe demand (recordings, messages, emails)
Documentation of work being held up
authority: Anti-Corruption Bureau (ACB) → Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) → CBI (central govt officials) → Lokpal/Lokayukta

scenario: Ponzi scheme, pyramid scheme fraud, multi-level marketing scam, chit fund fraud, collective investment scheme cheating, promised high returns and money not returned.
title: Ponzi Scheme / Investment Fraud
steps: Step 1: File an FIR at the economic offences wing of the police
Step 2: File a complaint with SEBI if securities or investments are involved
Step 3: File complaint with RBI if unauthorised deposit-taking is involved
Step 4: Register complaint with the Serious Fraud Investigation Office (SFIO)
Step 5: Join with other victims for a collective complaint for stronger impact
documents: All payment proofs to the company
provisions: Prize Chits and Money Circulation Schemes (Banning) Act 1978
authority: Economic Offences Wing → SEBI → RBI → Serious Fraud Investigation Office (SFIO)

scenario: Share market fraud, stock broker cheating, demat account manipulation, unauthorized trading in my account, broker not returning money, IPO allotment fraud.
title: Stock Market / Broker Fraud
steps: Step 1: File a complaint on SEBI SCORES portal (scores.sebi.gov.in)
Step 2: File a complaint with the stock exchange (NSE/BSE) where the broker is registered
Step 3: Approach the Investor Grievance Redressal Cell of the exchange
Step 4: File an FIR at the economic offences wing if fraud is large
Step 5: Approach the Securities Appellate Tribunal (SAT) if SEBI order is unsatisfactory
provisions: SEBI (Prohibition of Fraudulent and Unfair Trade Practices) Regulations
authority: SEBI SCORES Portal → Stock Exchange Arbitration → Securities Appellate Tribunal (SAT) → High Court

scenario: Mining on my land without permission, illegal sand mining near property, encroachment by mining company, environmental damage from nearby mine, groundwater damaged by mining.
title: Illegal Mining / Land Encroachment by Mining
steps: Step 1: File a complaint with the District Mining Officer
Step 2: Approach the State Pollution Control Board for environmental damage
Step 3: File an FIR for illegal mining and trespass
Step 4: File a complaint with the Central Empowered Committee (CEC) for forest land mining
Step 5: File a PIL in the High Court or approach National Green Tribunal
documents: Photographs and videos of mining activity
provisions: Mines and Minerals (Development and Regulation) Act 1957
authority: District Mining Officer → State Pollution Control Board → National Green Tribunal → High Court (PIL)

scenario: School not giving TC transfer certificate, school demanding capitation fee illegally, private school fees too high, school expelling student unfairly, RTE admission denied.
title: School Admission / Fee / TC Dispute
steps: Step 1: Send a written complaint to the school principal demanding resolution
Step 2: File a complaint with the District Education Officer (DEO)
Step 3: For RTE denial, file a complaint with the Block Education Officer under RTE Act
Step 4: For excessive fees, approach the State School Fee Regulatory Authority
Step 5: File a complaint with the State Human Rights Commission if child's rights are violated
documents: Written demand or rejection from school
Child's birth certificate and Aadhaar Card
Proof of residence for RTE admission (within 1km radius)
provisions: Section 12(1)(c) RTE Act (25% reservation in private schools)
Section 420 IPC (if capitation fee taken fraudulently)
authority: School Principal → Block Education Officer → District Education Officer → State RTE Authority → High Court

scenario: Ambulance not coming, emergency services not responding, fire brigade delayed, disaster relief not provided, NDRF not deployed, government failure in emergency response.
title: Emergency Services Failure / Disaster Response
steps: Step 1: Call 112 for all emergencies, 101 for fire, 108 for ambulance
Step 2: Document response time and any failure with photographs and written records
Step 3: File a complaint with the District Collector
Step 4: Write to the State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA)
Step 5: File a complaint with the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) if deaths occurred due to government failure
documents: Call records showing emergency calls made
Photographs and videos of emergency situation
Medical records if treatment was delayed
authority: District Collector → State Disaster Management Authority → NHRC → High Court

scenario: Forest land encroachment, tribal rights denied, forest dwelling community evicted, vanvasi rights violated, forest rights act claim rejected, jungle zameen claim.
title: Forest Rights / Tribal Land Dispute
steps: Step 1: Submit a claim under the Forest Rights Act to the Gram Sabha
Step 2: If claim is rejected, file an appeal to the Sub-Division Level Committee
Step 3: Further appeal to the District Level Committee
Step 4: Contact the State Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers Department
Step 5: Approach the High Court if all administrative remedies are exhausted
documents: Evidence of occupation before December 13, 2005 (photos, documents)
Aadhaar Card and caste certificate (for ST)
provisions: Scheduled Tribes and Other Traditional Forest Dwellers (Recognition of Forest Rights) Act 2006
authority: Gram Sabha → Sub-Division Level Committee → District Level Committee → High Court

scenario: Wrongful imprisonment, illegal detention by police, arrest without warrant, held beyond bail period, person missing after police custody, enforced disappearance.
title: Wrongful Detention / Illegal Arrest
steps: Step 1: Inform family members immediately if someone is arrested
Step 2: File a habeas corpus petition in the High Court for immediate production of the person
Step 3: File a complaint with the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)
Step 4: Approach the State Legal Services Authority for free legal aid
Step 5: File a complaint with the State Human Rights Commission
Step 6: Demand compensation for wrongful detention
documents: Arrest memo or grounds of arrest
provisions: Article 22 of Constitution (protection against arbitrary arrest)
Section 57 CrPC (person not to be detained beyond 24 hours)
D.K. Basu vs State of West Bengal guidelines
authority: Legal Aid Services → High Court (Habeas Corpus) → NHRC → State Human Rights Commission

scenario: NREGA wages not paid, MGNREGA work not given, 100 days work denied, job card not issued, work site corruption in NREGA scheme, NREGA payment into wrong account.
title: NREGA / MGNREGA Scheme Complaint
steps: Step 1: Register a complaint with the Gram Panchayat Secretary
Step 2: Approach the Programme Officer at the Block level
Step 3: File a complaint on the NREGA MIS portal (nrega.nic.in)
Step 4: File an RTI for information on your work and payment status
Step 5: Approach the District Programme Coordinator
Step 6: Contact Jan Suvidha Kendra or District Collector for resolution
documents: Bank passbook showing NREGA payment history
Aadhaar Card linked to job card
provisions: Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act 2005
authority: Gram Panchayat → Block Programme Officer → District Programme Coordinator → NREGA MIS Portal

scenario: PM Awas Yojana house not given, Pradhan Mantri housing scheme money not received, PMAY application rejected unfairly, housing scheme corruption, beneficiary list manipulation.
title: PM Awas Yojana / Housing Scheme Complaint
steps: Step 1: Check your application status on the PMAY portal (pmaymis.gov.in)
Step 2: File a complaint with your local Gram Panchayat or Urban Local Body
Step 3: Approach the Block Development Officer or Municipal Commissioner
Step 4: File a complaint on the public grievance portal (pgportal.gov.in)
Step 5: File an RTI to get information on beneficiary list and selection criteria
documents: Land document (for rural) or tenancy proof (for urban)
provisions: Article 19(1)(e) of Constitution (right to reside and settle)
authority: Gram Panchayat / Urban Local Body → Block Development Officer → District Collector → pgportal.gov.in

scenario: Scholarship not received, scholarship application rejected, merit scholarship denied, minority scholarship not given, post-matric scholarship delayed, NSP portal issues.
title: Scholarship Dispute / Non-Payment
steps: Step 1: Check your application status on the National Scholarship Portal (scholarships.gov.in)
Step 2: Contact the institution's scholarship coordinator
Step 3: File a complaint on the NSP helpline: 0120-6619540
Step 4: Contact the District Social Welfare Officer for state scholarships
Step 5: File an RTI to get information on scholarship disbursement status
documents: Bank account details linked to Aadhaar
authority: Institution Scholarship Coordinator → NSP Helpline → District Social Welfare Officer → State Scholarship Portal

scenario: Cow vigilante attack, mob lynching, communal violence, religious discrimination, forced religious conversion, demolition of religious structure.
title: Communal Violence / Mob Attack
steps: Step 1: Call 112 immediately if under attack or threat
Step 2: File an FIR at the police station immediately
Step 3: File a complaint with the National Commission for Minorities (NCM)
Step 4: Approach the State Human Rights Commission
Step 5: Contact the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)
Step 6: File a PIL in High Court if police are not acting
provisions: The Protection of Civil Rights Act 1955
authority: Police (112) → Police Station → National Commission for Minorities → NHRC → High Court (PIL)

scenario: Gender discrimination, transgender rights denied, LGBTQ+ discrimination, forced marriage, honour killing threat, denial of basic rights based on gender identity.
title: Gender Discrimination / LGBTQ+ Rights
steps: Step 1: Contact the National Legal Services Authority (NALSA) for legal aid
Step 2: Approach the State Transgender Welfare Board
Step 3: File a complaint with the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)
Step 4: File an FIR if physical violence or coercion is involved
Step 5: Approach the High Court for protection of fundamental rights
provisions: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India (2018 — decriminalisation of Section 377)
Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act
authority: State Transgender Welfare Board → NALSA → NHRC → High Court

scenario: Cybersecurity breach in company, data breach affecting customers, personal data leaked online, Aadhaar data exposed, company not protecting user data, PDPB violation.
title: Data Breach / Privacy Violation
steps: Step 1: Report the breach to CERT-In (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) at cert-in.org.in
Step 2: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 3: Notify affected individuals if you are the organisation responsible
Step 4: File a complaint with UIDAI if Aadhaar data is leaked
Step 5: Consult a cyber law specialist immediately
documents: Evidence of data breach (screenshots, email notifications)
Communication with the organisation about breach
authority: CERT-In → Cyber Crime Portal → UIDAI (Aadhaar data) → High Court

scenario: Wrongful tax demand, property tax overcharged, municipal tax assessed wrongly, house tax notice for wrong property, commercial tax demand on residential property.
title: Property Tax / Municipal Tax Dispute
steps: Step 1: Submit a written objection to the local tax assessment authority
Step 2: Request a revised assessment with correct property details
Step 3: Approach the Municipal Appeals Committee
Step 4: Pay tax under protest and file for refund if deadline is near
Step 5: Appeal to the Municipal Appellate Authority
Step 6: Approach the High Court for manifestly wrong assessments
authority: Local Tax Assessment Office → Municipal Appeals Committee → Municipal Appellate Authority → High Court

scenario: Cyber terrorism, hacking of critical infrastructure, ransomware attack on hospital or utility, government website hacked, critical information infrastructure attack.
title: Cyber Crime / Critical Infrastructure Attack
steps: Step 1: Report immediately to CERT-In at incident@cert-in.org.in or 1800-11-4949
Step 2: File a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in
Step 3: Preserve all logs and evidence before attempting recovery
Step 4: Report to the National Cyber Security Coordinator
Step 5: File an FIR with the cyber crime police
documents: System logs and evidence of attack
List of systems and data affected
provisions: IT Act Section 43 (Damage to computer system)
authority: CERT-In (1800-11-4949) → Cyber Crime Portal → Cyber Crime Police → National Cyber Security Coordinator

scenario: Illegal construction by neighbour, building without plan approval, encroachment on government land, violation of FSI/FAR norms, construction without commencement certificate, unauthorized colony.
title: Illegal Construction / Building Violation
steps: Step 1: File a written complaint with the local municipal building department
Step 2: File a complaint with the Town Planning authority
Step 3: Approach the District Collector if on government land
Step 4: File a complaint with the state RERA if it is an ongoing real estate project
Step 5: Approach High Court for demolition order if authorities are unresponsive
documents: Building plan of your own property (if affected)
authority: Municipal Building Department → Town Planning Authority → District Collector → High Court

scenario: Road rage incident, threatening behaviour by another driver, physical assault during traffic altercation, someone damaged my vehicle intentionally, rash driving causing danger.
title: Road Rage / Traffic Altercation
steps: Step 1: Do not engage with the aggressive driver — move away safely
Step 2: Note the vehicle number and take photographs if possible
Step 3: File an FIR at the nearest police station
Step 4: Get a medical examination if you were assaulted
Step 5: File for vehicle damage compensation through insurance or civil court
documents: CCTV footage from nearby cameras (request through police)
authority: Police Station → Motor Accident Claims Tribunal (for damages) → Consumer Forum (for vehicle damage)

scenario: Organ trafficking, forced organ donation, kidney sold under pressure, illegal organ transplant, hospital conducting illegal organ trade.
title: Organ Trafficking / Illegal Transplant
steps: Step 1: File an FIR immediately — organ trafficking is a serious cognizable offence
Step 2: Report to the state Appropriate Authority for Organ Transplantation
Step 3: Contact the CBI for interstate organ trafficking
Step 4: Report to the National Human Rights Commission
Step 5: Contact health ministry helpline
documents: Any documents related to organ donation
Bank records of payment made for organ
provisions: Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act 1994
Section 19 THOTA (Punishment for commercial dealing in organs)
authority: Police Station → State Appropriate Authority → CBI → NHRC

scenario: Bonded labour, forced labour, slave-like conditions, human trafficking for labour, migrant worker exploitation, workers not allowed to leave, wages withheld forcefully.
title: Bonded Labour / Human Trafficking
steps: Step 1: Call 1800-419-8588 (National Human Trafficking Helpline) immediately
Step 2: File an FIR at the nearest police station
Step 3: Contact the District Magistrate who has powers under Bonded Labour Act
Step 4: Approach the National Human Rights Commission
Step 5: Contact NGOs working on labour rights and human trafficking
documents: Any debt bond or agreement forcing labour
authority: National Helpline (1800-419-8588) → Police Station → District Magistrate → NHRC

scenario: Cow slaughter law violation, animal cruelty, pet dog or cat abused by neighbour, stray animal menace, illegal wildlife trade, poaching complaint.
title: Animal Cruelty / Wildlife Crime
steps: Step 1: Call the local SPCA (Society for Prevention of Cruelty to Animals) helpline
Step 2: File a complaint at the police station
Step 3: For wildlife crime, report to the State Forest Department or Wildlife Crime Control Bureau
Step 4: Contact PFA (People for Animals) helpline
Step 5: File a PIL in High Court for systemic animal cruelty issues
documents: Photographs or videos of animal cruelty
Aadhaar Card / ID proof of complainant
provisions: Prevention of Cruelty to Animals Act 1960
Indian Penal Code Section 428/429 (killing or maiming animals)
authority: Local SPCA → Police Station → State Forest Department → Wildlife Crime Control Bureau

scenario: College not recognising degree, degree from foreign university not accepted, UGC not recognising college, deemed university fraud, degree mill institution.
title: University / Degree Recognition Issue
steps: Step 1: Verify the institution's status on the UGC website (ugc.ac.in)
Step 2: File a complaint with the UGC if the institution is misrepresenting itself
Step 3: For foreign degrees, approach the Association of Indian Universities (AIU) for equivalence
Step 4: Approach the AICTE for technical education institutions
